Agentic Workspace · Plan → Build → Use → Evaluate

Edmonton Municipal Property Assessment — Analysis Workspace

A Claude Code workspace that helps municipal assessment analysts understand the City of Edmonton's official assessment records — reliably, explainably, and without ever estimating market value.
439,685 parcels5 skills (code-enforced) · 3 subagents · 2 workflows Two-tier governanceBuilt on the Day 1–3 method
Overview

What this is, and where each deliverable lives

An agentic workspace built around an Alberta (City of Edmonton) open dataset by running the course loop — Plan the living spec, Build the skills & subagents, put them to Use, and Evaluate with an independent critic. The dashboard is one read-only interface onto it, not the product.

Required deliverableWhere it is
1 · The workspace (CLAUDE.md, data, skills, subagents, workflows)§2–§5
2 · Evidence of use (outputs + run log)§7

1 · Opportunity & judgment Day 1Day 3 user story

A named person, a real decision — not a tour of the dataset

User story (top of the CLAUDE.md). Dana is a property-assessment analyst at the City of Edmonton. It is March, before assessment notices go out. Her lead asks: "How does Oleskiw sit against the city this year, and is anything in the west end worth a second look before we sign off?" Today that is half a day of spreadsheet work that hides the signal and is hard to defend. This workspace turns it into a five-minute, evidence-cited read. As an analyst, I want to summarise / compare / scan the official record with defensible numbers, so that I can decide what to review and trust before I sign off.

Primary — Assessment Analyst

Reviews and quality-checks the roll.

Compare neighbourhoods/wards, find records worth a closer look, summarise a scope defensibly.

Secondary — Manager & Tax-Planning team

Oversees the function / plans the base.

Honest one-page briefings and the base by ward/class, with coverage gaps stated.

Deliberately out of scope. Not for home buyers, investors, or realtors. No market value, no appreciation forecast, no purchase advice — the features the data allows but no municipal decision needs. They are banned, not built (the rejected "home-buyer valuation tool" is written down in the spec's convergence record).
2 · Living specification Day 1

The CLAUDE.md, written in the six-part shape

The spec the agent reads every run, structured exactly as Day 1 requires — each part carries a decision already made. Deletion test on every line: would removing it change the next run?

PartWhat it fixes
ObjectiveThe decision, user, leverage + the "no-dataset sentence".
What good looks like7 testable standards, a gold example, and a must-never list.
ScopeSmallest valuable version (4 capabilities) + one stretch (ward-portfolio) + explicit refusals.
DataOne row = one parcel; full record (not a sample); current-year only; points at the codebook.
CapabilitiesThe seam — what the agent decides vs. what is fixed in advance.
Human-in-the-loopFinal word + the override-logging rule (who/what/why, no log no change).
Gold example (the centrepiece the workspace must produce well — real numbers):
OLESKIW (Ward sipiwiyiniwak), Residential. n = 1,082. Median assessed value $762,250 (P25 $646,375 – P75 $914,750); CoV 0.56. Rank 9 of 291 comparable neighbourhoods. vs ward median $439,000: +$323,250; vs city median $393,500: +$368,750. Flagged high median ($762,250 exceeds the $728,625 fence). Official assessed values only — not market value; described, not judged or forecast.

Hard scope bans: no market value · no forecast · no causation · no owner/condition inference · no fabricated values (missing = "unavailable") · no judging an assessment right/too-high/too-low.

3 · Knowledge Day 1

The data connected as a live source — read, never pasted

The CSV (knowledge/data/property_assessment_current.csv, 71 MB) is read at run time by the deterministic compute layer (tools/property_compute_layer.ipynb), the only sanctioned source of numbers. Context is curated by the Deletion Test — only what the agent cannot guess: data-dictionary.md, data-profile.md (one row / scale / representativeness), data-cautions.md ("what the agent can't guess"), domain-notes.md, profile.json.

The distinction the workspace protects. The assessed value is the City's official mass-appraisal figure. The workspace reports and analyses it as an authoritative record — it never produces its own valuation, a current/future market price, or a forecast (none are in the data).
Data-quality conditionCount / valueHandled by
Missing ward8,893 (2.0%)relabelled (Ward not recorded), kept
Multi-ward tagging1,778own labelled row; flagged
Zero-value parcels50 at $0true min $0; drove the median-first rule
Extreme high valuemax $1.285Bshown; means never used alone
4 · Skills & infrastructure Day 2

Capabilities as code, not prose — "a prompt advises; a skill enforces"

Day 2's method: decompose the app into verbs, then give each the simplest form. The result — a capability-to-form map — with the connector deliberately left empty (local CSV in, local file out).

VerbFormWhere
Read the CSVNative actionpandas in the compute layer
Compute a scope's statisticsFixed pipelineproperty_compute_layer.ipynb → data.json
Summarise / compare / flag / briefSkillthe 4 business skills
Enforce scope & citation on a draftSkill (hard gate)governance-check
Get the numbers right / adversarially evaluateSubagentdata-analyst / workspace-critic
Reach a live outside systemConnector — empty on purposenothing live to reach mid-analysis

Every skill is a real Claude Skill: SKILL.md + a runnable scripts/*.py + a references/*.json, so the rule holds every run and was behavior-checked on a known input.

assessment-summary

Median-first summary of any scope. check: OLESKIW → $762,250, n 1,082, rank 9 ✓

neighbourhood-ward-comparison

Like-with-like deltas + rank. Karhiio vs Dene → −$60,500 ✓

anomaly-scan

Flags with rule + triggering number; a code check re-validates the fences. recomputed $728,625/$91,625 → MATCH ✓

executive-briefing

6 mandatory sections; self-runs the governance linter. OLESKIW briefing → governance PASS ✓

Subagents (one per decision worth isolating)

data-analyst

Owns computation.

Runs the compute layer, returns structured numbers with n — no prose.

governance-reviewer gate

Owns compliance (Day 2).

Runs check_claim.py on a draft; PASS or REVISE before a human reads it.

workspace-critic critic

Owns adversarial evaluation (Day 3).

Attacks the spec's claims, recomputes, returns findings for triage.

Two workflows chain the skills end-to-end: neighbourhood-review and ward-portfolio (summary → compare → scan → briefing → govern → human sign-off).

5 · Governance Day 2 gateDay 3 critic

Honesty enforced in code, then challenged by an independent critic

Tier 1 — the gate (during production)

Every draft passes governance-check: check_claim.py scans for scope bans, uncited figures, and a missing disclaimer, and exits non-zero to block the draft. A prompt only advises; this enforces.

bad draft → REVISE (4 findings); gold + all 4 run outputs → PASS.

Tier 2 — the critic (after the fact)

The workspace-critic subagent, fresh context, briefed to attack, role-plays Dana and tests two questions: does it serve her? and does it do what you think? Re-runnable → before/after.

critic_recompute.py → 12/12 claims CONFIRMED from the raw CSV.

The critic earned its keep. It found a real bug — asking a ward for a Non-Residential summary silently returned Residential figures, mislabelled. Triaged Accept → home: Skills → fixed (the skill now declines the unsupported class instead of fabricating). A second finding (an anomaly output missing its disclaimer) was caught by the linter and fixed. Both closed; one limitation deferred openly. Verdicts stay human — the critic does not get a vote (full record in governance/triage-record.md).

Decides autonomously: which figures to compute, rule-based ranking, which caveats to attach. Escalates to a human: whether a flag matters, any interpretation of why, and sign-off before anything leaves. Every accepted finding is given a home (Spec / Knowledge / Skills / Deliverables / Governance) so we fix the cause, not the symptom.

6 · Interface

The dashboard — one read-only window onto the workspace

Self-contained (data embedded, no network). Every panel maps to a user, a skill, and a decision, and carries a "Why is this shown?" explainer. It renders live below; static screenshots follow for offline review.

Live & interactive: switch tabs, search neighbourhoods, sort, and click a row to compare it to its ward and the city.

Screenshots

Overview — KPIs, the right-skewed distribution (why medians), and class mix; surfaces missing-ward (8,893) and $0 parcels honestly.
Ward Portfolio — residential medians by ward; amber rows are the multi-ward group and "Ward not recorded", so the 2.0% coverage gap stays visible.
Neighbourhood Explorer — WINDSOR PARK: rank #3 of 291, +$652,250 vs ward, +$694,250 vs city, with the no-market-value disclaimer.
Anomaly Watchlist — each flag with the rule and triggering number, plus the highest single residential assessments as a review list.
7 · Evidence of use

Real runs against the real data

Four representative runs (full outputs in runs/outputs/). Every figure is reproduced independently in §8, and every output now passes the governance linter.

Run 1 — comparison: Karhiio vs Dene (residential)

WardnMedianP25P75Δ vs Karhiio
Karhiio35,276$413,500$244,500$508,500—
Dene34,234$353,000$178,500$463,500−$60,500

Run 2 — anomaly scan: 12 high-median · 7 low-median · 30 high-dispersion, fences $728,625 / $91,625. Runs 3 & 4 — workflows: Oleskiw review (rank 9/291, governed briefing) and the ward-portfolio (all 13 wards, O-day'min surfaced as low outlier, coverage gap stated).

Run log — what I corrected. An early profile filtered value > 0 and hid 50 $0 parcels — fixed to keep all values (true min $0). The governance linter later caught the anomaly output missing its disclaimer — added. An early Oleskiw draft said homes were "worth ~$760k" — the linter blocked it (market-value rule) and it was rewritten to "official assessed value, median $762,250". What surprised me: O-day'min's residential median is only $126,500 — reported and flagged, cause not asserted.
8 · Evaluation Day 3

Testing the output — recompute, real before/after fixes, and the critic

Numbers are tested two independent ways; narratives are reviewed by hand (never model-scored). The before/after leads with two real fixes the loop caught, then a controlled baseline that quantifies why the conventions matter.

A · Independent recompute — 12 / 12 pass (evaluation notebook + critic)

CheckExpectedWorkspaceResult
City residential median (n)$393,500 (411,541)$393,500 (411,541)PASS
OLESKIW median & rank$762,250 · 9/291$762,250 · 9/291PASS
Missing / multi-ward records8,893 / 1,7788,893 / 1,778PASS
True minimum value$0 (50 parcels)$0PASS
High-median flags & fence12 · $728,62512 · $728,625PASS

B · Before / after — two real fixes the loop caught (primary evidence)

Genuine "ran it → found the flaw → fixed the workspace → re-measured" cases, not a constructed baseline.

FixHow it surfacedBeforeAfter
① $0 parcels maskedCompute-layer profiling (run log)An early profile filtered value > 0 → min reported $500, hiding 50 parcels at $0 (and 1,807 ≤ $500)All values kept → true min $0; the 50 zero-parcels surfaced & documented; recompute confirms 50
② Class silently mislabelledworkspace-critic edge probe (Day 3)assessment_summary --scope ward --class "Non Residential" returned Residential figures, labelled "Residential", with no warningWard/neighbourhood scopes now decline the unsupported class ("unavailable — re-run the compute layer") instead of fabricating
③ Anomaly output missing disclaimergovernance-check linterValuation-adjacent watchlist with no market-value disclaimerDisclaimer added; the linter now PASSES it

C · Controlled baseline — do the conventions actually matter?

A naive v1 (all-class mean, zeros dropped) scored against the governed v2 on a 5-point quality rubric across 4 scopes:

Rubric — before
0 / 20
Rubric — after
20 / 20
v1 "typical" (all-class mean)
$540,210
v2 "typical" (res. median)
$393,500

The naive shortcut overstated the "typical" assessment by ~37% ($540,210 vs the honest $393,500) — confirming the median-first / single-class / keep-all-values conventions are load-bearing, not cosmetic. That is exactly the failure mode Fix ① corrected in the real data.

Both real fixes were caught by the workspace's own instruments — the compute-layer profile and the re-runnable Day-3 workspace-critic (12/12 claims recomputed from the raw CSV) — and reviewed by a human before acceptance. Full triage in governance/triage-record.md.

9 · Write-up

Walking through the workspace

The user and the decision

The workspace serves Dana, a property-assessment analyst at the City of Edmonton. Her week is spent reviewing and quality-checking the assessment roll before notices go out. The trigger moment is concrete: in March her team lead asks how a neighbourhood sits against the city and whether anything nearby is worth a second look before sign-off. Today she answers by hand — pulling 439,685 rows into a spreadsheet, filtering to residential, remembering not to let one $19M parcel drag an average, re-deriving quartiles per neighbourhood, and eyeballing outliers — half a day that hides the signal and is hard to defend line by line. Two secondary users read her work: the assessment manager, who needs an honest one-page briefing, and the tax-planning team, who need the base by ward and class with its coverage gaps stated. It is deliberately not built for home buyers, investors, or realtors — the moment designed for is a municipal review, not a purchase.

The work it does

Four recurring questions map to four capabilities, each tied to a decision: what does the record say for this scope? (assessment-summary), how does it compare to peers? (comparison), what should I review first? (anomaly-scan), and give my manager an honest read (executive-briefing). The discipline is that a feature exists only if it maps to one of those rows. The workspace refuses the questions the data cannot honestly answer — market value, appreciation, owner attributes, causation — and states what it does hold instead of producing a clean but misleading number.

How it is built

Built by running the course loop — Plan, Build, Use, Evaluate — around one living spec. Knowledge: the CSV is connected as a live source and read at run time; the supporting notes are curated by the Deletion Test (only what the agent cannot guess — the $0 parcels, the missing / multi-ward quirks, the assessed-value ≠ market-value distinction). One deterministic compute layer owns every number, so any figure has exactly one place to audit. Five skills sit on top, each a real Claude Skill — a SKILL.md plus a runnable scripts/*.py and a references/*.json — so the rule is enforced in code, not advised in a prompt (the anomaly fences are re-validated in code; the governance linter exits non-zero on a violation). Three subagents isolate the decisions worth isolating: data-analyst (get the numbers right), governance-reviewer (keep words honest during production), and workspace-critic (attack the whole thing afterwards). A capability-to-form map records why each verb took the form it did, and why the connector form is deliberately empty.

What it produces

Evidence-cited summaries and comparisons, a prioritised anomaly watchlist where every flag carries the rule it triggered and the number behind it, one-page governed briefings, and a read-only dashboard that makes the same evidence explorable. Every output names its scope, its tax class, and its record count n; uses medians and quartiles as the headline; surfaces the relevant data-quality caveat; and carries the assessed-value ≠ market-value disclaimer. Nothing leaves without passing the governance gate and a human sign-off — the workspace prioritises, explains, and drafts; the analyst decides and owns the call.

What the evaluation showed

The numbers reproduce exactly — 12 of 12 headline claims recomputed independently from the raw CSV, by two separate instruments (the evaluation notebook and the re-runnable critic). More telling than the score were the two real fixes the loop caught: an early profile that masked 50 parcels assessed at $0, and a skill that silently returned residential figures under a non-residential request — both surfaced by the workspace's own instruments, fixed at the source, and re-measured. A controlled baseline confirmed why the conventions exist: a naive all-class-mean answer overstated the "typical" assessment by 37%. The honest reading is that the value of this workspace is not its arithmetic but its judgment encoded as enforced rules — and that the one open risk is provenance (municipal vs. provincial open data), flagged for confirmation rather than hidden.

10 · Distinctness statement

How this differs from my group's project

My group project, FireWatch, supported Alberta wildfire planning analysts on wildfire data. This workspace is a different dataset and problem: the City of Edmonton Property Assessment roll, for municipal assessment analysts understanding official assessment records. Different data, users, decisions, and content — this workspace is my own. What carried over is only the engineering philosophy — living spec, modular skills, human-in-the-loop, governance and explainability as first-class, transparent thresholds, and before/after evaluation.

Provenance note (raised honestly). The assignment specifies a "Government of Alberta" open dataset. Edmonton Property Assessment is published on the City of Edmonton open-data portal — Alberta municipal open data rather than the provincial portal. The domain is clearly distinct from FireWatch; I recommend confirming acceptability with the instructor. Every figure is computed from the provided dataset — nothing fabricated or external.
Edmonton Property Assessment Workspace · MMA 616 Final · built on the Plan → Build → Use → Evaluate method · all figures computed from property_assessment_current.csv (439,685 records) · decision-support, not prediction.
